issue_title: Index not properly recreated on `Null` to `Not Null` migration
issue_body:
When running migrations, if a field is altered from being nullable to not allowing nulls, any indexes for it are not recreated or dropped, which causes an error. The exact error should look like below (with field names substituted):

```
django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: ('42000', "[42000] [Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][SQL Server]The index '<INDEX_NAME>' is dependent on column '<COLUMN_NAME>'. (5074) (SQLExecDirectW)")
```

To work around this issue, we had to drop the index manually (using alter field and setitng `db_index` to `False`), alter the field to no longer be nullable, then alter the indexing back to true to create the index. This should, however, be handled by default.

---
**Old Migration:**
```python
migrations.AlterField(
    model_name='<MODEL_NAME>',
    name='<FIELD_NAME>',
    field=models.ForeignKey(related_name='+', to='<RELATION_NAME>', null=False),
),
```

---
**New Migration:**
```python
migrations.AlterField(
    model_name='<MODEL_NAME>',
    name='<FIELD_NAME>',
    field=models.ForeignKey(related_name='+', to='<RELATION_NAME>', null=True, db_index=False),
),
migrations.AlterField(
    model_name='<MODEL_NAME>',
    name='<FIELD_NAME>',
    field=models.ForeignKey(related_name='+', to='<RELATION_NAME>', null=False),
),
migrations.AlterField(
    model_name='<MODEL_NAME>',
    name='<FIELD_NAME>',
    field=models.ForeignKey(related_name='+', to='<RELATION_NAME>', null=False, db_index=True),
),
```

```python
    @detail_route(methods=['post'])

        """
        Endpoint for allowing a provider to accept a request.
        ---

        response_serializer: snowremovalservice.serializers.RequestSerializer
        parameters_strategy: replace

        parameters:
            - name: pk
              description: Primary key of the request to be accepted.
              required: true

however, since this is a detail_route the *pk* parameter must be part of the request path, so the Swagger documentation must be updated to something like this:

```python
    @detail_route(methods=['post'])
    def accept_from_provider(self, request, pk=None):
        """
        Endpoint for allowing a provider to accept a request.
        ---

        response_serializer: snowremovalservice.serializers.RequestSerializer
        parameters_strategy: replace

        parameters:
            - name: pk
              description: Primary key of the request to be accepted.
              required: true
              type: int

issue_title: TypeError: build_attrs() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
issue_body:
Hi, I'm experiencing the below error using django v1.10.2:

```
Django Version: 	1.10.2
Exception Type: TypeError
Exception Value: build_attrs() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
Exception Location: .../bootstrap_datepicker/widgets.py in render, line 115
```

The culprit seems to be the below line as `build_attrs` doesn't expect `attrs`:
```
input_attrs = self.build_attrs(attrs, extra_attrs)
```

Replacing the above with:
```
self.attrs = attrs
input_attrs = self.build_attrs(extra_attrs)
```
seems to work.

If I try to use RedisManagedJWTAuth instead of JWTAuth, I got this error:
__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'refresh_lock'

Seems this is because RedisManagedOAuth2Mixin calls parent class (JWTAuth) with extra argument (refresh_lock=), which is absent in that class, but present in grand parent class (OAuth2).

issue_title: Make sure that when the OQ database is dropped also the datastore is cleaned
issue_body:
We should provide an utility to help the users to cleanup the database and the datastore, which destroy the database and removes /home/*/oqdata.

Previously it was enough to run a `dropdb openquake2`. Now also the datastore in the users' home needs to be cleaned to avoid clash with calculation IDs.

This issue has been triggered by some server/cluster users and also because the datastore feature is not documented and happened also on the VMs for the Risk course.

Sorry for the non-bug, I didn't know where else to ask this trivial question.

EC2 create_image creates an Amazon EBS-backed AMI from an Amazon EBS-backed instance that is either running or stopped. One of its required parameters is image name:

> [REQUIRED]
>

>

issue_title: fix_s3_hosts always assumes you're using central aws instead of a local S3.
issue_body:
I've noticed when attempting to generate presigned urls that the logic behind fix_s3_hosts and the virtual styling always assumes you're using AWS S3. [fix_s3_host](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/6065566237de957507fc63ae4d3a034c87552adf/botocore/utils.py#L649)

However, we have a local s3 api on top of ceph, as such it throws away our local s3 endpoint and substitutes s3.amazonaws.com, although the presigned urls work when you switch out the virtual path for our local s3, it's a pain.

This is because the default_endpoint_url passed to [switch_to_virtual_host_style](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/6065566237de957507fc63ae4d3a034c87552adf/botocore/utils.py#L656) is always set to 's3.amazonaws.com'

The request object is available in the fix_s3_host function so it should be a fairly simple fix.

issue_title: Why do we need to specify key in `botocore.signers.generate_presigned_post`?
issue_body:
## The Issue

The function signature found here https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/master/botocore/signers.py#L600

reads
```
def generate_presigned_post(self, Bucket, Key, Fields=None, Conditions=None,
                            ExpiresIn=3600):
```

which would imply that the `Key` is mandatory when generating a presigned POST policy. However the [AWS reference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/sigv4-HTTPPOSTConstructPolicy.html) doesn't seem to have that constraint. 

## My Motivation

I'm asking this here because I'd like to generate a POST policy for mobile clients that will allow them to perform multiple uploads without the need to request a new policy per upload. My thought being that I can do this fairly easily by only enforcing a "starts-with"  condition on the S3 key. So my policy would look more like the example policy AWS gives in the above reference doc:

```json
{ "expiration": "2007-12-01T12:00:00.000Z",
  "conditions": [
    {"acl": "public-read" },
    {"bucket": "johnsmith" },
    ["starts-with", "$key", "user/eric/"],
  ]
}
```

E.g, I could use this set of conditionals to generate a POST policy which a mobile client could use to upload a whole album of photos.

## TL;DR

When including `Key` as a required argument in the function signature of `generate_presigned_post`, it implies I need to generate a new presigned S3 post for every additional client upload which is bothersome / impossible if I want to do batch uploading.

So I am requesting that become a kwarg. 

Let me know if I'm making sense. Especially let me know if I'm just an idiot who missed something. That happens a lot with me.

Hello.

Looking at [ReadTheDocs > Available Services > APIGateway > Client > create_deployment](https://botocore.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/apigateway.html#APIGateway.Client.create_deployment), it reads as follows for the variables Request Parameter:

> [...] Variable names can have alphanumeric characters, and the values must match [A-Za-z0-9-._~:/?#=,]+ .

Unless I am misreading, the variable names should be ONLY alphanumeric characters (eg: A-Za-z0-9).
However, I used the API to succesfully deploy to a stage with the following variable name: **stage_storageprefix**.

So, I think the document might be wrong, or maybe the regex it includes applies to both variable names and values (instead of just the values as it seems to suggest).

I think the botocore docs in this regard are piped from the AWS API Reference. The relevant snippet _might_ be [this one](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/api-reference/resource/stage/#variables)... but maybe you have a way to submit request for docs clarifications or changes to the mothership?

Clearly this is not a blocking issue for me, more of a curiosity I followed-up on because, after writing code to validate the alphanumeric range, I was able to add that stage variable via the AWS Web Console, and wanted to figure out where the inconsistency came from.
Thanks in advance

issue_body: My url "vm_test_1.hsg.test" is considered invalid by botocore. After replacing underscores with hyphens like this "vm-test-1.hsg.test" it works fine. I have read that google advises to use hyphens over underscores, however, I don't think that urls with underscores should be considered invalid.
Python 3.6 got released, and some distro (like Fedora) are swithcing to it.

It would be nice to have operations that allow you to do things like
* concat_batch: concatenate multiple expressions into a single batched expression
* pick_batch_elements: pick only a subset of the elements from a batched expression

I'm trying to train an LSTM with minibatches. I've been using the [tutorial example](https://github.com/clab/dynet_tutorial_examples/blob/master/tutorial_rnnlm_minibatch.py) which is very helpful. I have a question about the masking, which I think should be different in my use case. In the tutorial, the loss is computed at each timestamp (when predicting the next word), and the loss is masked. In my use case, I use only the last LSTM state, feed it to an MLP and compute the loss once. 

I had another idea: for each input I feed to the LSTM, if some of the instances are shorter than the current timestamp (and have zeros in the current mask, accordingly), it just pushes forward their previous states. Something like the following:

```python
mask_matrix = np.tile(mask, (1, max_length))
curr_state = mask_matrix * new_state + (1 - mask_matrix) * curr_state
```
This is just to exemplify, it of course doesn't work because it tries to multiply a matrix with a state. Is there something similar or a possible workaround? Does this make sense at all to do it like that?

Thanks in advance!

Easy way: call `to_dask_array(lengths=True)`. This will take some computation.

The harder (maybe not too hard) way to do this would be to directly support dask dataframes.

Fit clones of an estimator on each block / partition, and ensemble the results somehow http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.ensemble.VotingClassifier.html, http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.ensemble.BaggingClassifier.html#sklearn.ensemble.BaggingClassifier, http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.ensemble.BaggingRegressor.html#sklearn.ensemble.BaggingRegressor

issue_title: PV clock drift causes more ceph havok
issue_body:
I am running nothing but CoreOS 607 instances now, in both of my environments.

While load testing our 3-node staging cluster, the cluster was a bit slow. Looking into the problem, I noticed that one of these PV based instances had drifted 10-15 seconds into the future. I turned up ntp and synced the times. Not more than 15 minutes later, two of our instances became non-responsive. After rebooting both instances from the AWS console, we stopped the load testing, and I spent a couple of hours piecing back together the fleet units that had fallen over - repairing ceph in the process.

issue_title: Consider enumerating etcd peers instead of using a discovery URL
issue_body:
The etcd discovery service is meant only to bootstrap a new cluster, and there's no guarantee that the endpoint will work indefinitely. This can prevent users from adding new hosts to an existing CoreOS cluster. We should switch from a discovery service to manually specifying  

To do this, we'll need to edit [user-data.example](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/contrib/coreos/user-data.example) to replace the discovery URL with a list of etcd peers. We'll also need to specify IP addresses on each cloud provider so the hosts come up with a known IP address. We should be able to do this on AWS by disabling DHCP, but other providers may be more difficult.

issue_title: Automated release testing on all cloud providers
issue_body: Manually testing Deis releases on the various supported platforms is 1-2 days of manual work for the entire Deis team. We have [test-ec2](https://ci.deis.io/job/test-ec2/) now successfully validating changes and releases against EC2 - we should investigate doing this for all cloud providers. Saving this manual effort is a big win.

**Problem**
I am trying to host a Prestashop. I'll try to refrain myself from judging and will stick to objective facts, but Prestashop is a pile of crap. Basically, it does not allow for any kind of abstraction and does really stupid things with image files (idiotic rewriting rules, non-configurable storage directory, ...).

In order to adapt this to the 12-factor concepts, I need to store those images in an external service, like S3. This way, images are automatically available from everywhere. Especially with something like yas3fs which handles caching files and mounting the same bucket from several locations.

**Solution**
What would be interesting would be to be able to attach another app (or something similar) as Docker Volume Plugins that will be started alongside the main app, with the same config (so you can get credentials from environment).

Something like

```
cd plugin-app
deis create --storage-plugin plugin-app
git push origin master

cd ../main-app
deis create main-app volume_name:/app/img plugin-app
deis config:set TOKEN=xxxxxx
git push origin master
```

issue_title: Automatically build release EBS snapshots for AWS
issue_body:
At Engine Yard, we create EBS snapshots of the Docker volume for each Deis release. We then use this snapshot to create new volumes for hosts that we create. 

We should have a CI job that kicks off this automatic build and snapshot creation so that we can share it with our community.

/cc @iancoffey

issue_title: Azure: VM's not being created and IP not set

issue_body:
Interface layers should be able to declare other layers as requirements.

At a minimum, foo-interface could declare a requirement on foo-layer. foo-layer can provide a wheelhouse.txt and specify layer options to get the base layer to install required packages.

It would be nicer if foo-interface could declare its dependency directly on the base layer, and provide a wheelhouse.txt, packages in layer.yaml, required config.yaml options etc. It could be composed exactly like any other layer.

Perhaps the cleanest solution but not backwards compatible is to drop the distinction between interface layers and normal layers completely. The differences seem limited - interface layers just get hooks automatically generated, and that change will go away once https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/issues/98 is implemented.

issue_title: support for max_time as an alternative to max_tries
issue_body:
Full jitter is great, but it can mean that whatever you're polling gets significantly less time than you might expect to be ready.

It would also be great to be able to say something along the lines of "try for 5 minutes, backing off exponentially".

In the [movielens example](https://github.com/lyst/lightfm/tree/master/examples/movielens), should we be doing 
```
train_precision = precision_at_k(model, train, k=10).mean()
test_precision = precision_at_k(model, test, k=10, train_interactions=train).mean()

train_auc = auc_score(model, train).mean()
test_auc = auc_score(model, test, train_interactions=train).mean()
```
I haven't looked through all the code, but I think if `train_interactions=None` then most of the top K recommendations are going to be movies that the user has viewed in the training set, and thus not in the test set.

Making this change bumps test precision from `0.11` to `0.11` and test AUC from `0.90` to `0.93`.

~ Ben

Hi,
not really an issue. I am new to LightFM. Before I used implicit for generating recommendations but there was no build-in option for passing in user- / item-features.

Implicit defines a function on the model for getting similar items. Does adapting this function to LightFM work? I think I'm missing the theoretical background to see for myself.


```
def similar_items(self, itemid, N=10):
    scores = self.item_factors.dot(self.item_factors[itemid]) / self.item_norms
    best = np.argpartition(scores, -N)[-N:]
    return sorted(zip(best, scores[best] / self.item_norms[itemid]), key=lambda x: -x[1])
```

https://github.com/benfred/implicit/blob/master/implicit/als.py

I know how to use lightfm, but I don't know how to split datasets when using it in first real case
is there have a documents show that?

issue_title: Manually adding an editable package would cause locking to fail
issue_body:
Locking requires every sdist to run `python setup.py egg_info` beforehand to produce a `*.egg-info` directory. `pipenv install -e .` does this, but if you write your TOML manually, you will get this error.

##### Steps to replicate

```python
# setup.py
from setuptools import setup
setup(name='hello', version='1.0')
```

```toml
# Pipfile
[packages]
hello = { path = ".", editable = true }
```

```bash
$ ls -a
Pipfile setup.py
$ pipenv --three
(snipped)
$ pipenv lock
(will produce the exception below)
```

##### Expected result

Locking finishes successfully with my editable package in the lock file.

##### Actual result

```
  File "pipenv/patched/piptools/resolver.py", line 278, in _iter_dependencies
    for dependency in self.repository.get_dependencies(ireq):
  File "pipenv/patched/piptools/repositories/pypi.py", line 246, in get_dependencies
    legacy_results = self.get_legacy_dependencies(ireq)
  File "pipenv/patched/piptools/repositories/pypi.py", line 264, in get_legacy_dependencies
    dist = ireq.get_dist()
  File "pipenv/patched/notpip/_internal/req/req_install.py", line 966, in get_dist
    egg_info = self.egg_info_path('').rstrip(os.path.sep)
  File "pipenv/patched/notpip/_internal/req/req_install.py", line 562, in egg_info_path
    'No files/directories in %s (from %s)' % (base, filename)
pipenv.patched.notpip._internal.exceptions.InstallationError: No files/directories in hello (from )
```

------

The following will produce the expected result:

```
pipenv run python setup.py egg_info
pipenv lock
```

so essentially we just need to run `egg_info` for local packages to ensure they are initialised correctly. I think.

issue_title: pkg_resources hard-fails on install_requires for entry point.
issue_body:
This has bitten us a few times now, I think that `pkg_resources` is a bit aggressive about `install_requires`, so when you attempt to invoke an entry point, the `install_requires` list is checked, and if any of the dependencies are missing, the script fails to start.

This problem has come up because we have a system that side-loads pre-built source (a la DPKG and presumably other package managers) where the dependencies are declared by the package manager (so `install_requires` is actually mostly ignored at install time). Occasionally we have needed to change the name of the package deployed by the package manager *even though the importable path has not changed*. If someone has the old package name in their `install_requires` and invokes their script via `python -m mypkg`, it works fine, but if they are invoking through an entry point, the script *fails to run* because the dependencies are not installed.

To demonstrate, I have prepared a [small repository](https://github.com/pganssle/pkg_resources_install_requires_demo) that demonstrates this behavior.

This is a real problem because it occurs *at runtime* and there doesn't seem to be any obvious way to "shim" around it other than by providing a dummy package (with no easy way to notify users that they are relying on this dummy package).

I suggest changing the `DistributionNotFound` error into a warning instead.

Hello,

https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/885

in the past few days I broke my system (Ubuntu 16.04) python with the above bug. I have already fixed my system, but it's really more of a hack than a fix. I don't know what caused it. 

This is what I tried to fix it:

 * the whole apt package remove install reinstall. Didn't work.

 * next step was uninstalling and then tracking down and rm/deleting every version of the related packages, pip, pkg_resources and setuptools that I could find my in my system path.

 * Because I still had everything else, I thought I could run the get-pip.py to get pip back, which surprisingly also didn't work, despite the file being last edited in November 2016 and the above issue was closed earlier, so I assumed whatever fix was devised was in there. It threw the same error anyway, except the trace was pointing to some tmp directory which I assume is where the unpacked contents of get pip go.

## What worked:

* cloning the repos for setuptools and pip

* setuptools' bootstrap.py installing setuptools and pkg_resources

* installing pip manually with the setup.py in the pip repo

The obvious disadvantage is that by installing manually apt is not aware of these packages now. That's the hacky part I'm a bit worried about.

Everything else seems to work perfectly now, so props to the people designing that modularity.

If you kind devs could double check that the get-pip.py is working correctly and maybe that the apt packages install the correct setuptools version with the fix (I think my old one was 20. something), that would be great.

I hope you don't consider this spam. Have a great day!

issue_title: The -p N.M shortcut doesn't work for Python 3.5 on Windows
issue_body:
The ```--python``` command line can take an abbreviated interpreter name. On Unix this is one of the versioned alias names (```python2.7```, ```python3```, ...) and is looked up on ```PATH```.

On Windows the versioned names don't exist so the registry is searched for the Python version specified. So, ```-p 2.7``` finds the location of Python 2.7 in the registry and uses that.

However, the registry layout is not well documented, and is complex (user installs, 32-bit vs 64-bit). It also seems to have changed in Python 3.5, and ```-p 3.5``` does not work. Rather than attempt to fix this, though, I propose that the feature be marked as deprecated, and left as it stands. It's not clear that it was ever widely used.

People wanting to use an abbreviated interpreter name would be better to run ```py -N.M -m virtualenv``` (assuming virtualenv is installed under Python N.M) or ```py -N.M C:\path\to\virtualenv``` to run a standalone copy of virtualenv. This uses the (fully supported) Python launcher to select the required Python version.

issue_title: [ACR] Repository list command doesn't support table output
issue_body:
**UPDATE:** I just noticed that the `az acr repository show-tags` command has the same behavior as described below.
---

Running `acr repository list`, while the CLI is configured to use table output (which is the new default?), outputs the following error:

`Table output unavailable. Use the --query option to specify an appropriate query. Use --debug for more info.`

If I append `--query "repositories"`, then the table output will work, so I have to at least give the CLI props for the nice error message :) However, it's non-ideal to need to view the JSON output of the command, in order to understand the schema, and then add a `--query` flag in order to "pluck" the repositories list out of the returned object. 

Maybe I'm doing something wrong, but I've not encountered another list command in the CLI that didn't naturally support table output, so it would be cool if `acr repository list` did as well.

Is this already functionality that is available in the CLI? If so, I may just be missing something.

issue_title: Low BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE leads to performance hits for max_connections=1
issue_body:
When using azure.storage.blobservice.get_blob_to_file, the algorithm will download a 64MB (or smaller) file in one call REST call.

However larger files are chunked in 4MB blocks.  For a 65MB file this would result in 17 calls, which significantly decreases performance.  For my application, changing _BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE to be the same as _BLOB_MAX_DATA_SIZE (64MB) improved performance drastically.

Not sure of all use cases to know if this will impact other applications, but if it cannot be hard coded to 64MB, perhaps allowing a chunk_size  argument would help.

issue_title: UserPassCredentials: support ws-trust flow for federated AAD account
issue_body:
The pertinent code is [here](https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_active_directory.py#L405-L413), per the latest suggestion from AAD group, for federated accounts we should use ws-trust to acquire tokens from ADFS on-prem instead of directly posting the request to the AAD endpoint, which will not work in new tenant.
To fix this:
1. Use ADAL
2. Port the logic from ADAL
3. Deprecate this 

//cc: @lmazuel

issue_title: Buggy $LINES and $COLUMNS not being set properly
issue_body:
When opening the first Guake tab, `$LINES` and `$COLUMNS` aren't set to reflect the size of the actual terminal, and are set as the default `24` and `80`, respectively.

I'm using Fish shell, and here is how to identify the issue. Add the following to the init file (`~/.config/fish/config.fish`):

```fish
echo "Size: $LINES x $COLUMNS"
```

Then opening the terminal gives the following output, even when the terminal is of a different size:

```fish
Size: 24 x 80
$> echo "Size: $LINES x $COLUMNS"
Size: 38 x 239
$> 
```

Observe that the variables are set correctly _after_ config files are loaded, which causes issues when running programs in init files that care about the terminal size.

Using a signal hook, I could observe the reason for this bug. Guake is sending `SIGWINCH` to the terminal, but after an unacceptable delay that causes issues with init scripts. `~/.config/fish/config.fish`:

```fish
echo "Size: $LINES x $COLUMNS"

function signal-hook --on-signal SIGWINCH
  echo "Size: $LINES x $COLUMNS"
end
```

And opening the terminal again:

```fish
Size: 24 x 80
$> Size: 38 x 239
$> 
```

The signal handler is triggered after some time (less than 1 second) showing the correct values.

Interestingly enough, none of these issues occur when creating a _new_ tab after this already happens to the first tab.

issue_title: Wrong config update when using named config and nested ingredients
issue_body:
This issue I believe is related to #290. I am using Sacred's master branch and get a key error of config entry not being used anywhere when setting a nested ingredient config while using a named config. Here's a minimal code:

```python
# sample.py

from sacred import Experiment, Ingredient


foo = Ingredient('foo')

@foo.config
def foo_cfg():
    a = 10


bar = Ingredient('bar', ingredients=[foo])

@bar.config
def bar_cfg():
    b = 20


ex = Experiment(ingredients=[foo, bar])

@ex.config
def default():
    c = 20

@ex.named_config
def named():
    c = 30

@ex.automain
def main():
    pass
```

Running `python sample.py print_config with named foo.a=40 -f` gives

```
INFO - sample - Running command 'print_config'
INFO - sample - Started
Configuration (modified, added, typechanged, doc):
  c = 30
  seed = 890348833                   # the random seed for this experiment
  bar:
    b = 20
    foo:
      a = 40
  foo:
    a = 40
INFO - sample - Completed after 0:00:00
```

Note that there is another `foo` inside `bar`'s config. That causes the error when no `-f` is passed.

issue_title: VUnit python interface: adding files to a Library
issue_body:
# Problem
The [vunit.ui.VUnit.library](https://vunit.github.io/python_interface.html#vunit.ui.VUnit.library) method returns [new instances of a library](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/b5b9b2498a9150632b3ea0f8f701526fdc5f1c32/vunit/ui.py#L416). I understand that this is just an interface into the underlying `vunit.project.Library` class, but this becomes an issue when trying to access previously added libraries on an instance of VUnit programatically. For a bit more context, I am setting up a larger test suite using the pattern @LarsAsplund lays out on [SO](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42980036/how-to-combine-multiple-vunit-run-py-files-into-a-single-vunit-run). When doing this I need to "get" Libraries I may have previously created and added files to so I can add additional files. The problem seems to be with how `vunit.ui.Library.add_source_file` interfaces with the underlying project.

# Reproduction
```python
from vunit import VUnit
from os.path import dirname, abspath, join

# create the file we try to add
open('file.vhd', 'a').close()

proj_root = dirname(abspath(__file__))
vu = VUnit.from_argv()

try:
    test_lib = vu.library('Test')
except KeyError:
    print('Test Library does not exist, creating it now.')
    test_lib = vu.add_library('Test')

test_lib_2 = vu.library('Test')

print('Test Library returned after initial creation       : {0} - {1}'.format(test_lib.name, test_lib))
print('Test Library returned after "getting" it from VUnit: {0} - {1}'.format(test_lib_2.name, test_lib_2))

test_lib.add_source_file(join(proj_root, 'file.vhd'))
test_lib_2.add_source_file(join(proj_root, 'file.vhd'))

# Cannot access file because we it got added to the same library twice.
for file in test_lib.get_source_files('file.vhd'):
    print('Name: {0} - Library: {1}', file.name, file.library)
test_lib.get_source_file('file.vhd')
```

There are some Mimetypes in the ZipPreviewBuilder that have not been checked.
It could happen that with some kind of compressed file the error "file is not a ZipFile" would be raised.

Let's decompose what happened here:

1. I created an environment with IPython 4.2 and Python 3.5. You can't see it here, but the reason I included Python 3.5 was that `conda create -n test ipython=4.2 pytest` defaulted to Python 2.7 (even though my conda is itself in Python 3.5).

2. I converted the environment to Python 2.7 with `conda install python=2.7`. This chose IPython 1.0 (!) from my channel. 

3. I fixed this by installing `ipython=4.2` manually.

4. `ipython` was then broken, because of a too old version of six, 1.3. At this point *both* `conda install six` and `conda update six` did nothing. I had to manually conda install six=1.10 to update it. 

The final command reverted back to Python 3.5, which seems to have worked correctly. 

So there are a few bugs here:

- conda didn't pick the latest version of Python when I created the environment. I know that this sort of thing is technically possible in the solver because newer versions of one package require older versions of another, but there are so many versions of Python that this seems unlikely. Is it possible the way the solver picks the best solution changed? It should be picking latest versions, then fewest packages to break ties. 

- Downgrading Python picked very old versions of some dependencies, for now apparent reason.

- Updating IPython didn't update its dependencies. There's a reason the [update_dependencies](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/config.html#disable-updating-of-dependencies-update-dependencies) option was set to default to true. 

- Both `conda install six` and `conda update six` didn't update six. I would expect at least one, if not both to do it. I believe it used to work that install always installed it, and update would skip it if the package was already the latest version (the difference being if a dependency might also be updated otherwise).

issue_title: Partial permissions errors in conda install leads to partially-installed package
issue_body:
Observed behavior: numba team had a docker image for building.  Docker image installed miniconda as root, but changed permissions on install folder to 777.  At some point, some install or update may have changed those permissions, leading to the lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda folder not being writable by the user.  However, the bin folder was writable.

conda update conda ran "successfully" - but updated only the activate scripts, not the conda folder.  Since the new conda 4.1.2 activates things with an extra argument, the old conda code was barfing.

We probably need to fail and roll back if any part of a package fails.

issue_title: Test for virtualenv fails for conda env
issue_body:
Can someone help me understand how conda envs differ from those created by `virtualenv`? 

I ran across [this code](https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil/blob/master/Makefile#L185) that checks for an active virtualenv,

```
check_venv:
        @$(python) -c 'import sys; sys.exit( int( not hasattr(sys, "real_prefix") ) )' \
                || ( printf "$(red)A virtualenv must be active.$(normal)" ; false )
```

I have no idea why this requirement is enforced but this check fails if using conda to manage python environments.

I would like to modify this test to work with conda env as well.

issue_title: Unsatisfiable deps error when building a package that has features
issue_body:
When building a package that both has features and has tests (run_test.sh or run_test.py exists in the recipe dir), conda fails to correctly bootstrap the test environment, reporting unsatisfied dependencies. The following script will reproduce the problem:

``` bash
mkdir fail

cat > fail/meta.yaml << EOF
package:
  name: fail
  version: 1

requirements:
  build:
    - python

  run:
    - python

build:
  features:
    - debug

EOF

touch fail/build.sh
touch fail/run_test.sh

conda build fail
```

If the `touch fail/run_test.sh` line is removed, or the debug feature is removed, the build runs successfully

I'm running conda 3.18.5 and conda-build 1.18.1

I am able to workaround the issue by adding the following line to my ".condarc":

``` yaml
track_features: debug
```

issue_title: config param 'disallow' is non-functional in conda 4.4
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior For `conda >=4.4,<=4.4.7` the `disallow` configuration parameter is non-functional. Its usage was removed as part of https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4158 in https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/4158/commits/19e69d1d567aeb71811eb637b16408e7cd7a2c20. Additionally, in `conda 4.3.*`, `disallow` is only considered for explicitly user-requested packages but not for dependencies. The parameter's description does only say `Package specifications to disallow installing` but not that it doesn't apply for dependencies. ### Steps to Reproduce ``` (base) $ conda --version conda 4.4.7 (base) $ conda config --describe disallow # disallow (sequence: str) # string delimiter: ',' # Package specifications to disallow installing. The default is to allow # all packages. # # disallow: [] ``` ``` (base) $ conda --version conda 4.4.7 (base) $ CONDA_DISALLOW=mkl conda install --dry-run mkl Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/maba/code/conda/conda-4.4 added / updated specs: - mkl The following packages will be downloaded: package | build ---------------------------|----------------- mkl-2018.0.1 | h19d6760_4 184.7 MB intel-openmp-2018.0.0 | hc7b2577_8 618 KB ------------------------------------------------------------ Total: 185.3 MB The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: intel-openmp: 2018.0.0-hc7b2577_8 mkl: 2018.0.1-h19d6760_4 (base) $ CONDA_DISALLOW=mkl conda install --dry-run numpy Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/maba/code/conda/conda-4.4 added / updated specs: - numpy The following packages will be downloaded: package | build ---------------------------|----------------- libgfortran-ng-7.2.0 | h9f7466a_2 349 KB intel-openmp-2018.0.0 | hc7b2577_8 618 KB numpy-1.14.0 | py36h3dfced4_0 4.0 MB mkl-2018.0.1 | h19d6760_4 184.7 MB ------------------------------------------------------------ Total: 189.6 MB The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: intel-openmp: 2018.0.0-hc7b2577_8 libgfortran-ng: 7.2.0-h9f7466a_2 mkl: 2018.0.1-h19d6760_4 numpy: 1.14.0-py36h3dfced4_0 ``` ``` (root) $ conda --version conda 4.3.32 (root) $ CONDA_DISALLOW=mkl conda install --dry-run mkl CondaValueError: specification 'mkl' is disallowed (root) $ CONDA_DISALLOW=mkl conda install --dry-run numpy Fetching package metadata ........... For simple name-only specs like with `CONDA_DISALLOW=mkl` that behavior can be emulated with unsatisfiable pinned specs like `CONDA_PINNED_PACKAGES='mkl<0,>0'`: ``` (base) $ CONDA_PINNED_PACKAGES='mkl<0,>0' conda create --dry-run -n env numpy Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/maba/code/conda/conda-4.4/envs/env added / updated specs: - numpy The following packages will be downloaded: package | build ---------------------------|----------------- pip-9.0.1 | py35h7e7da9d_4 2.2 MB setuptools-38.4.0 | py35_0 525 KB wheel-0.30.0 | py35hd3883cf_1 67 KB openblas-0.2.14 | 4 3.6 MB libgfortran-3.0.0 | 1 281 KB python-3.5.4 | h417fded_24 28.3 MB certifi-2017.11.5 | py35h9749603_0 196 KB numpy-1.10.2 | py35_0 5.8 MB ------------------------------------------------------------ Total: 40.9 MB The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: ca-certificates: 2017.08.26-h1d4fec5_0 certifi: 2017.11.5-py35h9749603_0 libedit: 3.1-heed3624_0 libffi: 3.2.1-hd88cf55_4 libgcc-ng: 7.2.0-h7cc24e2_2 libgfortran: 3.0.0-1 libstdcxx-ng: 7.2.0-h7a57d05_2 ncurses: 6.0-h9df7e31_2 numpy: 1.10.2-py35_0 openblas: 0.2.14-4 openssl: 1.0.2n-hb7f436b_0 pip: 9.0.1-py35h7e7da9d_4 python: 3.5.4-h417fded_24 readline: 7.0-ha6073c6_4 setuptools: 38.4.0-py35_0 sqlite: 3.20.1-hb898158_2 tk: 8.6.7-hc745277_3 wheel: 0.30.0-py35hd3883cf_1 xz: 5.2.3-h55aa19d_2 zlib: 1.2.11-ha838bed_2 (base) $ CONDA_PINNED_PACKAGES='zlib<0,>0' conda create --dry-run -n env numpy Solving environment: failed UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict: - numpy -> python=2.7 -> zlib[version='>=1.2.11,<1.3.0a0'] - zlib[version='<0,>0'] Use "conda info " to see the dependencies for each package. ``` ## Environment Information

We are using an UUID Binder. The object the UUID is refering to does still exist, but the UUIDBinder has some constraints that this object does not contain anymore. In hindsight, one should not add such a filter. In hindsight
Some of the forecast tests are not very reliable due to issues with the data feed (I think). Some combination of mocking, allowing failures, and more granular testing is needed in that module.

``` python

    def build_Getattr(self, o):

issue_title: RPM rsync distributor test should assert repository layout
issue_body: The rsync distributor test should assert that the published repository on the remote server has the same layout as the repository published by the yum distributor. The repository should contain 2 directories: repodata and Packages. Packages directory should contain a directory for each letter of the alphabet for which there is a package that starts with that letter. Each letter directory should contain packages whose names start with that letter.

issue_title: Test publish yum repo with different checksums
issue_body:
1. create an "rpm" repo in pulp with one or more RPMs, SRPMs, and DRPMs.
2. publish it with the "sha256" checksum type
3. verify publish (see below)
4. publish it with the "sha1" checksum type
5. verify publish (see below)
### Verification

It depends on how fancy and robust you want to get when it comes to inspecting the published XML. You'll need to somehow look at primary, other, filelists, and prestodelta XML files and make sure all of the unit-related checksums are using the checksum type you asked for.

Smash could download the files and actually parse them as XML. That wouldn't be too difficult, but who likes parsing XML...

Smash could just ensure that the string "sha256" shows up in the case when it should, and doesn't when it shouldn't.

In the case of "other" and "filelists", the checksum type itself won't be included. Each package will have a `pkgid` attribute that is the checksum value of the specified type. You could just verify that it's the correct length. Or you could actually cross-reference it with the checksum for the corresponding package listed in primary.

Another general way to help with this verification is to just make sure yum and/or dnf can actually read the metadata and use it. In my manual testing, I published a rhel7 kickstart repo and kickstarted a VM from it, just to make sure it was generally sane.

2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

    return convert(image, np.uint8, force_copy)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/skimage/util/dtype.py", line 205, in convert
    raise ValueError("Images of type float must be between -1 and 1.")
ValueError: Images of type float must be between -1 and 1.
```

I tried to implement the layer on gluon like this:
```
class BilinearInterp(nn.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, target_size, **kwargs):
        super(BilinearInterp, self).__init__(**kwargs)
            self.height = target_size[0]
            self.width = target_size[1]
    def forward(self, x):
        return mx.nd.contrib.BilinearResize2D(x, self.height, self.width)
```

But I've got error msg:

> Argument data must have NDArray type, but got (Symbol icnet0_batchnorm0_fwd)

Is it possible to implement gluon interpolation layer?
I tried a different approach (mx.nd.GridGenerator + mx.nd.BilinearSampler), also unsuccessfully.

issue_title: Automatic context to select CPU or GPU(s)
issue_body:
This is a feature request.

It would be very convenient to have MXNet automatically detect if a GPU is available and use it.  Or if there is more than one available, use all GPUs.  This would avoid a lot of wasted boiler-plate code where people have to specify the context manually, which is usually hard-coded or sometimes passed in from the command line.

Certainly there are times when you don't want to use all the available hardware.  Either if you're sharing hardware with others, or in those cases where MXNet doesn't yet scale out well.  But it's super common to just want to use whatever the machine has -- especially in cloud computing environments.

issue_title: test_operator.test_bilinear_sampler has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_title: A bug in an example in the python API document
issue_body:
This is an example found in the doc for [mxnet.autograd.Function](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/autograd.html#mxnet.autograd.Function).
```python
class sigmoid(Function):
    def forward(self, x):
        y = 1 / (1 + mx.nd.exp(-x))
        self.save_for_backward(y)
        return y

    def backward(self, dy):
        # backward takes as many inputs as forward's return value,
        # and returns as many NDArrays as forward's arguments.
        y, = self.saved_tensors
        return y * (1-y)
```
The `backward` method should return `dy * y * (1-y)` instead of `y * (1-y)` according to the chain rule.

issue_title: repeat run constructor function of SymbolBlock when using gluon interface
issue_body:
## Description
when convert `mx.sym.##op##()` to `SymbolBlock` and run forward() during training, i found it will repeat call the constructor function, so if we Initialize some variable in the constructor() and change it in forward(), then it will be wrong because every time after it changed, it will be Initialized again.

give an example here: 
https://github.com/luoyetx/mx-lsoftmax/blob/master/operator/lsoftmax-inl.h#L54-L70

`next_beta_` will be assign as `param.beta * 0.1f` every time before forward(), so we'll never go into https://github.com/luoyetx/mx-lsoftmax/blob/master/operator/lsoftmax-inl.h#L178  

when using no gluon interface(original mxnet symbolic interface), it works well, but if we changed to gluon(SymbolBlock), then no work.

## Environment info (Required)

python
ubuntu16.04
mxnet-v1.0.1

@piiswrong  @sxjscience

issue_title: Assimp generates headers at compile time
issue_body:
Assimp generates `config.h` from `config.h.in` at compile time. When building cmake out of the source tree, this means `config.h` does not end up in the `include/assimp` directory at all. 

On Visual Studio, all cmake projects build in `%userprofile%\CMakeBuilds`. 

When using Assimp as a dependency, it's useful to have it in a git submodule. However, because `config.h` gets generated at compile time, and is not inserted into the source directory, any program using Assimp will fail to compile, because of the missing include. 

The obvious solution would be to build Assimp in the source tree, but depending on the environment (e.g. Visual Studio) this is not always possible.

issue_title: Add support for Docker v2 registries
issue_body:
I'm here via Saltstack, and troubleshooting a issue with connectivity while doing a pull. The problem is that the library is hardcoded to reference the v1 API to verify SSL connectivity to a local registry, which does not work with v2 registry.

The request should be changed to also try and ping `https:// + hostname + "/v2/"` in addition to `'https://' + hostname + '/v1/_ping'`

The rest of the pull stream works OK, since it is connecting to Docker, not the repo directly otherwise.

See: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/auth/auth.py#L32

issue_title: Feature Request: some API should support image list as args
issue_body:
For example, you can use 

```
docker save -o images.tar.gz docker.example.com/images/{image1,image2}:latest
```

 to save multi docker at one time ( [docker source code](https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/020a86b3d9e6b052a3f866b7517eb0f62fd24bea/api/server/router/image/image_routes.go#L172) )

However, docker-py do not support multi images as api args.

I write one by my self, 

```
class ImageApiMixin(object):
    ......
    def get_image(self, images):
        images_query = "?names=" + "&names=".join(images)
        res = self._get(self._url("/images/get" + images_query), stream=True)
        self._raise_for_status(res)
        return res.raw
```

so I can use

```
client.get_images([image1, image2])
```

to save multi images to one file。

issue_title: [kumo] use special role for cloudformation deployments
issue_body:
There is a new Feature in CloudFormation which lets a User specify a Role which shall be used to execute the Stack.
This would allow us to reduce users rights, but allow them to handle everything via cloudformation.

Docs can be found at http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/APIReference/API_CreateStack.html  (Parameter RoleARN)

At a first glance this seems easy to implement as optional config option.

issue_title: Many commands in Mobly are run through system shell, leading to quoting, cross-compatibility and injection issues.
issue_body:
Many commands in mobly (like all the adb commands and standing subprocess commands) are run through the system shell. The subprocess documentation advises against this because it is brittle (the command has to handle shell interpolation/quoting rules which might also vary between shells) and dangerous (because it makes shell injection vulnerabilities possible).

We should move away from using the system shell whenever possible and instead execute commands directly by supplying an argument list to 'subprocess'.

## Summary
Objects that override `__getattr__` to provide [dynamic access to a cffi](https://github.com/jcupitt/pyvips/blob/master/pyvips/vimage.py#L741) (such as [pyvips](https://github.com/jcupitt/pyvips)) are not supported by fire. ## The problem(s)
### Tracking Metadata state
Fire adds state to the object passed in (see [fire.decorators](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/master/fire/decorators.py#L82)). I'd argue that fire may see benefits in terms of reducing code complexity and deliver predictable behavior for _any and all_ python objects if this state were tracked separately. A simple dictionary `{ class: metadata }` stored internally to fire may be sufficient, though there may be better ways.

To demonstrate what I mean, look at what happens when I change the script to the one below, we see progress, but still no working result.
```python
import fire
from pyvips import Image

if __name__ == "__main__": 
    item = Image.new_from_file
   default = {
        fire.decorators.ACCEPTS_POSITIONAL_ARGS: not inspect.isclass(Image),
    }
    setattr(item, fire.decorators.FIRE_METADATA, default)
    fire.Fire(item)
````

It would appear that making calls to methods that return additional callable objects is not well supported by fire, as evidenced by the stack traces above.

Then further down the image_format is set again from the command line arg without a default making the first line redundant?

Perhaps the second time image_format is set it should be pulling from metadata?

I believe this is why I needed to set the format in https://github.com/mapbox/mbutil/pull/89/files#diff-3eb4d995269f8dd7de58cb236d151d7bR57 rather than letting it default to what it found in metadata.json.

There appears to be an API bug when looking up a Payment by payment ID. The response seems to end up with the current datetime as the `create_time` and `update_time` fields, including inside of the Sale resource under `related_resources` in `transactions`.

However, if you take the sale ID from inside `related_resources` and lookup the Sale directly, it has the correct `create_time` and `update_time`.

Here's a code example of the bug:

````Python
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> payment = paypalrestsdk.Payment.find("PAY-XXXXX")
>>> nested_sale = payment.transactions[0].related_resources[0].sale
>>> sale = paypalrestsdk.Sale.find(nested_sale.id)
>>> nested_sale.create_time == sale.create_time
False
>>> nested_sale.update_time == sale.update_time
False
>>> nested_sale.create_time                 
u'2016-02-06T15:20:40Z'
>>> sale.create_time
u'2015-12-29T21:34:15Z'
>>> datetime.utcnow()  # Current UTC time should be just after the create_time
datetime(2016, 2, 6, 15, 21, 9, 934184)
````

issue_title: Race when refreshing dependent resources can cause invalid snapshots
issue_body:
Because the engine signals that it has completed processing an event before it commits the result of the event to the snapshot, it is possible today for a step depending on another step to start, complete, and commit itself to the snapshot before the step on which it depends does so.

A real example of this:
```
I0807 14:27:26.811892   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pulumi:providers:pkgA::default (0)
I0807 14:27:26.811901   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resA (0)
I0807 14:27:26.811908   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resA (1)
I0807 14:27:26.811915   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pulumi:providers:pkgA::default (1)
I0807 14:27:26.811921   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resB (0)
I0807 14:27:26.811926   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resB (1)
I0807 14:27:26.811932   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resC (0)
I0807 14:27:26.811938   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resC (1)
I0807 14:27:26.811945   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resD (0)
I0807 14:27:26.811952   66794 lifecycle_test.go:106] same urn:pulumi:test::test::pkgA:m:typA::resD (1)
```

Because the Go scheduler did not schedule the goroutine handling pkgA::default for 23 microseconds, `resA` began, end, and committed itself to the snapshot before pkgA::default had the chance to do so.

We should signal completion of a step only after we commit the step to the snapshot.

issue_title: 2.2 regression: can not bind member function with lifted access
Allow running the test suite against the installed pybind11
issue_body:
Now that `pybind11` installs a corresponding CMake package configuration, it would be nice to make the test suite a standalone CMake project, which can be built against either an exported or an imported `pybind11` target. One could then run the following:

```
apt install pybind11-dev
cd $<path-to-test-suite>
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. && make && make check
```

This would help with CI on Linux distributions (for instance the `debci` infrastructure on Debian), and help catch future integration issues, like the gcc-6 LTO regression experienced in #604, much earlier in the development cycle.

It appears that NumExpr does not accept Python 3 string arrays, regardless of how they are sanitized before evaluation.

Consider the following code:

```python
import numpy as np
import numexpr as ne
x = np.array(list('abcdefg'), dtype='S1')
ne.evaluate("where(x == 'd', 10, 5)")
```

On Python2.7, this evaluates to `array([ 5,  5,  5, 10,  5,  5,  5])`, but on Python3.6 a `TypeError` is raised:
```

C:\ProgramData\Miniconda3\lib\site-packages\numexpr\necompiler.py in precompile(ex, signature, context)
    564

--> 566     constants_order, constants = getConstants(ast)
    567 
    568     if isReduction(ast):

C:\ProgramData\Miniconda3\lib\site-packages\numexpr\necompiler.py in getConstants(ast)
    334     constants_order = [const_map[v] for v in ordered_constants]
    335     constants = [convertConstantToKind(a.value, a.astKind)
--> 336                  for a in constants_order]

Is there any support for strings in Python 3? Or am I missing some kind of conversion beforehand?

NumPy versions:
 - Python3:  v1.12.1.
 - Python2: v1.9.2rc1

NumExpr versions:
 - Python3: v2.6.2
 - Python2: v2.4

issue_title: Broken options class for pandas.io.data - Did Yahoo just change their options site?
issue_body:
Hello,
Yahoo seems to have changed their site design as I no longer can pull any options data with panda.

Does anyone else experience this?

Here's what's called:
#!/usr/bin/python
import sys, getopt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from pandas.io.data import DataFrame
from pandas.io.data import Options
import h5py as h5
from pandas_datareader import data, wb
import datetime
import time
ticker = str(sys.argv[1])
new_data=pd.DataFrame()
option = Options(ticker, 'yahoo')
data = option.get_all_data()
new_data = new_data.append(data)
new_data.to_csv('cache/'+ticker)

And here's what I am getting:

Traceback (most recent call last):
File "db1.py", line 18, in 
data = option.get_all_data()
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas/io/data.py", line 1134, in get_all_data
expiry_dates = self.expiry_dates

`Table.update_or_insert()` and `Table.validate_and_update_or_insert()` should use `_for_update=True` when checking whether the record already exists. Otherwise there is a risk that the record will be deleted or (if it does not exist) inserted by a concurrent thread, leading to either database error or inconsistent data.

Adapters which don't support `for_update` locking should just silently ignore the parameter instead of raising exception (or at most log a warning).

The current layout of the [**Grid** class](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.core.Grid.html#pyart.core.Grid) is historical and ad-hoc, it does not follow a formal standard.  A more formal layout of the class should be used which conforms to the [CF conventions](http://cfconventions.org/).  The section of the CF convention which are relavent are [5.6 Horizontal Coordinate Reference Systems, Grid Mappings, and Projections](http://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-conventions/cf-conventions-1.6/build/cf-conventions.html#grid-mappings-and-projections) and [Appendix F. Grid Mappins](http://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-conventions/cf-conventions-1.6/build/cf-conventions.html#appendix-grid-mappings).

A specification for [Gridspec](http://www.gfdl.noaa.gov/~vb/gridstd/gridstd.html#gridstdse2.html) standard has been put forward as a possible method for describing gridding data within the CF conventions.  This standard is aimed at storing model data but may be adaptable for storing gridding radar data.

There is also the [UGRID Conventions](https://github.com/ugrid-conventions/ugrid-conventions) for specifying unstructured grids.
It's not obvious from the code/tests if it works at all.

issue_title: Raise a warning when a keyword passed to `get_output()` is unused
issue_body:
`lasagne.layers.get_output()` can be called with keyword arguments, which are passed to all traversed layers. This makes it possible to control layers with multiple behaviours (e.g. dropout, which can be turned on or off, or batch normalization, which can use minibatch statistics or aggregated statistics).

The most common use case for this is the `deterministic` keyword, which is used to turn off any regularizing noise in the net at test time (i.e. dropout).

The handling of these keywords is implemented in the `get_output_for()` method of each `Layer` class, which is required to take a catchall `**kwargs` argument to absorb any keywords that the layer doesn't recognize.

One issue with this is that a `Layer` instance might unintentionally ignore a certain keyword, and this is basically a silent failure. If you misspell `deterministic` as `determinstic` for example, all layers will ignore it and no warning or exception will be raised.

This is not very user friendly. Recently I had a brief discussion about this issue on this reddit thread: https://www.reddit.com/r/MachineLearning/comments/3xom8a/discussion_what_would_you_like_to_see_in_a/cy6hqg8

One of the developers of climin ( https://github.com/BRML/climin ) chimed in and pointed me to their solution for this issue: https://github.com/BRML/climin/blob/master/climin/util.py#L140-L151

Basically, check the called function's argument specification through introspection, and see if any arguments were provided that aren't listed there. If so, raise a warning.

I think such a solution may be possible for Lasagne as well. We could have `get_output()` inspect the argument specification for all `get_output_for()` calls it makes, and keep track of the set of all accepted keyword arguments across all layers. Once the graph is traversed, we can check whether any of the provided keyword arguments are not in this set.

As far as I can tell this would only require modifications in `get_output()` itself. It would complicate the code somewhat, but not excessively so, and I think the usability benefit should not be underestimated.

There is one big caveat: not all `get_output_for()` implementations specify all keywords arguments they accept in the method signature. Some implementations work directly with the `kwargs` dictionary, most importantly the new batch normalization implementation (#467).

So we'd have to figure out a workaround for that. The simplest approach would be to always have all accepted keyword arguments in the method signature, but I don't know if that is desirable. Another solution would be to provide an alternative way for such methods to 'declare' which keyword arguments they understand, but I think that might get a little too complicated.

I might try whipping up a PR for this, but before I do that we should probably figure out what to do with accepted keyword arguments that are not explicitly declared in the method signature.

issue_title: Announcement: You can training lasagne model in DIGITS now.
issue_body:
I'm happy to announce that I have made some modifies to DIGITS to support training lasagne model.

You need install [deepstacks.lasagne](https://github.com/guoxuesong/deepstacks) and [a modified version of DIGITS ](https://github.com/guoxuesong/DIGITS). You still need to install caffe, because DIGITS need it.

Please download mnist dataset following [DIGITS's GettingStarted document](https://github.com/NVIDIA/DIGITS/blob/master/docs/GettingStarted.md). When creating model, select Deepstacks.lasagne instead of caffe. Click 'customize' of LeNet, and paste the following code:

```
# Example for using raw lasagne:

import deepstacks
import lasagne
from deepstacks.framework.main import *
import deepstacks.framework.using_lmdb
from deepstacks.macros import *
from deepstacks.framework.macros import *
def build_network(inputs):
    network = inputs['image']
    if 'mean' in inputs:
        network=lasagne.layers.ElemwiseMergeLayer((network,inputs['mean']),T.sub)
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(
            network, num_filters=20, filter_size=(5, 5),
            nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify,
            W=lasagne.init.GlorotUniform())
    network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(network, pool_size=(2, 2))
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(
            network, num_filters=50, filter_size=(5, 5),
            nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify,
            W=lasagne.init.GlorotUniform())
    network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(network, pool_size=(2, 2))
    network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(
            lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=.5),
            num_units=500,
            nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify)
    network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(
            lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=.5),
            num_units=10,
            nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.softmax)

    res, stacks, paramlayers, errors, watchpoints=deepstacks.lasagne.build_network(network,(
        (classify,'target'),
        ),inputs)
    return res,stacks,paramlayers+[network],errors,watchpoints
```

'Show visualizations and statistics' and 'Classify Many' feature are not supported yet.

Enjoy it.

issue_title: Speeding up training on images of different sizes
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm training on a natural scene dataset whose images are different sizes. I'm using several convolutional layers followed by a layers.dnn.SpatialPoolingDNNLayer to standardise the sizes before the fully-connected layers.

As far as I can see, this means I have to fit the network one image at a time, since storing images as a batch means they would all have to be the same size. Is this assumption correct?

Unfortunately, running batches this way slows down fitting by a factor of about 50 - which is not surprising, since we're running the images one by one.

Can you think of a way to speed this up - or is this issue better approached in Theano?

Thanks,
Fintan

Two issues which might need to be in MDS but I am not certain.
 1. How to associate calibration data with actual data
 1. How to associate background data with actual data

Both these are issues because they might be performed at any point during your total beamtime but need to be associated with all your data. Also the calibration/background might be different for different samples/runs. Finally, calibration data is most useful in the form of (at least for XPD) a dictionary of distances and detector angles, it would be very tedious (without an auto calibrator) to need to calibrate every time you touch your data. Thus it would be great to store this processed data with the calibration data somehow.

Thoughts?

issue_title: algolia_reindex command resets the ranking formula.
issue_body:

issue_title: How to convert between pixel & sky?
issue_body:
What approach will be the default for converting between pixel and sky?  Probably all of these should be implemented, but one should be the default:

 * convert all regions to polygons, convert sky coordinates to pixel and vice versa
 * keep circles, rectangles, etc. "fixed", convert by assuming the projection is perfectly flat

Contained in option 1 is a question about how accurately we need to discretize continuous shapes when converting to polygons.

Also, which regions will get "abstract" representations allowing them to be rotated and manipulated with simple parameters?  e.g., ellipse, rectangle have major/minor axes and angles that are their minimal representations, but these are not preserved through projection.

issue_title: poor choice of virtualhost model when creating example configuration file
issue_body:
#### Context and Background
- I am running a server with multiple virtual hosts, some on port 80 and some on port 443.
- I did    `./letsencrypt-auto certonly`
#### Observed Behavior:

On the terminal screen:

```
 - Congratulations! Your certificate and chain have been saved at
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/xxx.av8n.com/fullchain.pem.
```

In my server configuration directory, a new file:

```
:; cat default-b-le-ssl.conf
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
<VirtualHost *:443>
        ServerName xxx.av8n.com
        DocumentRoot /var/www/xxx
        Include             /etc/apache2/sites-available/generic.conf
SSLCertificateFile insert_cert_file_path
SSLCertificateKeyFile insert_key_file_path
Include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-apache.conf
</VirtualHost>
</IfModule>
```

This example file was evidently modeled on my pre-existing file `default-b.conf` which is the configuration for the virtual host on port 80.
#### Desired and/or Expected Behavior

It would be incomparably better for the newly-created example file to be modeled on the existing virtual host on port 443 (rather than 80).
#### Additional Suggestions
- Having the `./letsencrypt-auto certonly` command scribble in my configuration directory was not expected ... but it is arguably desirable.  Some changes are needed to make iit desirable, to make it useful.
- The contents of the file need to be improved:  rather than saying `insert_cert_file_path` it should incorporate the actual path to the actual file.  The script certainly knows the correct path.
- Ditto for `insert_key_file_path`
- An even more important improvement:  To obtain the desired server behavior, the chain.pem file is required.  The example file should contain all three, not just the first two:

```
        SSLCertificateFile      /etc/letsencrypt/live/xxx.av8n.com/cert.pem
        SSLCertificateKeyFile   /etc/letsencrypt/live/xxx.av8n.com/privkey.pem
        SSLCertificateChainFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/xxx.av8n.com/chain.pem
```
- There are ways of consolidating this to two directives, but leaving it unconsolidated works fine.
- By the same token, the "congratulations" message on the controlling terminal should mention all the relevant .pem files, not just fullchain.pem.
- The "congratulations" message should mention that the example file has been created and can be taken as a suggestion for how to configure the server.  This is required by basic UI design principles. It is utterly pointless to create this file if the user doesn't know it exists.
- The documentation for   `./letsencrypt-auto certonly`  should tell the user to expect this file to be created.  Again, this is a basic UI design issue.
#### Details

```
:; git show HEAD
commit de182946b5cf09a3486f38d3df0c43e2280b3654

:; ./letsencrypt-auto --version
Updating letsencrypt and virtual environment dependencies......
Running with virtualenv: /root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt --version
letsencrypt 0.0.0.dev20151104

:; uname -a
Linux cloud 3.11.4+ #11 Fri Oct 11 05:39:29 PDT 2013 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

:; lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS
Release:        14.04
Codename:       trusty
```

This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-nsone` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_nsone.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: Invalid datetime can produce partial parse. Should throw ValueError instead
issue_body:
I came across this silent failure when parsing a third party datetime with an unusual separator:

```python
>>> import ciso8601

>>> ciso8601.parse_datetime('2017-05-29 17:36:00Z')
datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 29, 17, 36, tzinfo=<UTC>)

#Now for an out of spec string (invalid separator)
>>> ciso8601.parse_datetime('2017-05-29_17:36:00Z')
datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 29, 0, 0)

```

It seems that the parser sees a character that it doesn't recognize and stops parsing?

I would have expected that if the entire string cannot be parsed, it would raise a `ValueError`, not silently return a datetime without time or `tzinfo`. 

This lead to a sneaky bug in the code.

issue_title: Exclude ignores wildcard on folder names
issue_body:
I'm trying to use cpplint to check some Arduino code. I'm using `--recursive` to find all source files in numerous sub-directories. However, this is causing it to pick up the auto-generated code that the Arduino preprocessor creates in `.build` directories.

Per the `--help` documentation, I believe I should be able to direct the tool to ignore these folders with a call like:

    cpplint --recursive --exclude=.build src

However, this does not work, and cpplint still attempts to parse the auto-generated (and messy) Arduino code. I've also tried:

    cpplint --recursive --exclude=*/.build src

as well as other wildcard variations, but nothing seems to work.

The docs say `--exclude` supports globbing, but if that were so, I would expect one of these patterns to work. Am I misunderstanding it, or is this a limitation of cpplint?

Thanks for sharing this! I'm trying to write some unit test coverage (which is running in CI, so I'm mocking out the calls to pagerduty) but am having a rough time figuring out how to generate a mocked out `Incident` object. For a list of Incidents, I was trying something along the lines of:

```
 Incidents(Incident(html_url='pagerduty.com/theincident', incident_key='this was a page'))
```

Since it looks like when you request a list of incidents they are wrapped in an `Incidents` (aka a `Collection`). I'm curious if the community already has a pattern for doing this, but if not I'll come back around in a few weeks with a path- either a test fixture, example, or something depending on what everyone prefers.

Thanks!
Joe

issue_body:
If a request (client.index in my case) causes Transport error, trying to print the error results in 

    TypeError: string indices must be integers

from /home/micha/retresco/nlg-backend/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/elasticsearch/exceptions.py:55:

     cause = ', %r' % self.info['error']['root_cause'][0]['reason']

where self.info['error'] is a string ('MapperParsingException[...' in my case).

The snapshot status method only reports the status of snapshots being created. In order to see the state of a snapshot being deleted you need to make calls to the delete method and catch/inspect the exception. I'd like to see a similar method to status for getting the status of deletes as well as creates. Currently the only way to test whether a delete is still running is to try another delete and catch the exception. This is particularly problematic if you are scripting cleanup operations since only one delete can run at a time. It leads to some very ugly code where you are forced to catch exceptions for successful calls or to get a status.

issue_title: Change notifications not working for Bytes traitlet
Union trait does not produce correct info_text for Instance traits
issue_body:
When using Instance traits within a Union trait their info_text seems to get lost:
```python
import traitlets as tr

tr.Union([tr.Instance(int)]).info()
# returns 'any value'

tr.Instance(int).info()
# returns 'an int'
```

This is not really an issue, but I wasn't sure where to post it.  With [PEP 487](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0487/) accepted into Python 3.6, it might be worth thinking about how traitlets can benefit if at all.

issue_title: Entering space infront of assignment name unfetchable
issue_body:
* Ubuntu 16.04
* jupyterhub --version 0.8.1
* jupyter --version 4.4.0
* nbgrader version 0.6.0.dev (Also referred to as Formgrader)

Replicate:
Create an assignment with a name with space in front, like: " V1-T1"
Generate and release works as expected and all the foldernames have "\ " in the foldernames infront(like in the source/ and srv/nbgrader/exchange/) but the Formgrader does not show the space in front.

The TA that made this assignment did not realize that he made this error when writing the assignment name in the dialog. But the nbgrader database had the assignment name with the space in front but the list in the Formgrader did not show the space.

Possible solution / easy fix:
This could be fixed just by using pythons strip() when validating the string when the user makes the assignment because probably no one wants to have a space there anyways.

## Issue
In situations like tutorials, tutorial leaders often provide jupyter notebooks to be followed along with while also using a master notebook themselves. Oftentimes these master notebooks can be subjected to changes and the changes are not easily legible in the tutorial environment. A tutorial participant being able to view that notebook live on their machine from a website would be of huge use to those struggling to keep up with changes to a master notebook. 

## Proposal
I'm not entirely sure how it would work. I have talked to someone who has had this issue and wrote a shell script to continuously `git add` `git commit` the master notebook he is working on, while users can keep refreshing the rendering of the github hosting of the notebook. Maybe there are some technical limitations, but wrapping the entire rendering of the master notebook in an iframe would prevent viewers of the master notebook from using it (I think?) This master notebook would have to be hosted somewhere, and be able to communicate with the instance of the master notebook being live edited by a tutorial teacher. This is really beyond my skill level.

Anyways, this feature would be really nice, I was encouraged by some Cal Poly students to post an issue and I hope it makes sense!

Is it possible to access the various elements of a notebook from code inside that notebook?

For instance, using the IPython kernel, you can do a very limited kind of introspection using such special variables as `In` and `Out`.

It would be useful to have full access to all cells (code, markdown, raw), their sources, all parts of output, metadata, etc.

In particular, `In` contains a _mangled_ version of the actual input (code cell source). Magics have been replaced by calls to `get_ipython().run_cell_magic()`. With `_i` you seem to get an _unmangled_ version of the input of the most recently executed code cell (but is there a mechanism to get this for arbitrary code cells?).

Can this be achieved through JavaScript?

The reason I want this, is that we are developing notebook test frames that contain holes, which will be filled by material from a notebook that students fill. The test frame needs access not only to the execution result (e.g. as left behind in variables), but also to the actually code itself for some static tests that query the code's Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
